channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Blood Dungeon is a Refreshing Spin on the Survivors Formula
item.content:encoded:
I love to throw on a podcast or long-winded YouTube video on one screen while doing a run or five on Vampire Survivors. Well, I've gone and completed that game 100 percent, but I think I just found a worthy replacement.

Blood Dungeon is the latest title from developer Messhof, most known for creating the Nidhogg series. Blood Dungeon is familiar for fans of Survivors-like, but it throws in a few twists that makes it feel fresh and different.

During Summer Game Fest Play Days, I had the pleasure to check this title out. The good news is, there's a public demo available, so if you like what you hear, definitely give it a go. As a bonus, I played it on Steam Deck and it worked spectacularly.

For a game of this genre, you still have your standard auto-attacking that you'd expect, but Blood Dungeon is also a platformer. For as much as I love Vampire Survivors, it's very one-dimensional.

Here, on the other hand, we have a whole new layer to contend with as far as movement is concerned. Your character can climb walls, either hanging on the side or from above. Hang from steel grates, or monkey your way across chains. Avoid damage by any means, while dishing it out with increasingly powerful attacks.

Enemy waves gradually ramp up in intensity. Stronger enemies, more enemies, they'll be thrown in your path. Constant movement is key, while also collecting the blood of fallen enemies to level up.

Level-ups will grant you upgrades to your weapons, which range anywhere from pistols and machine guns to noxious gas clouds and spider webs.

The spider web weapon is one I want to pay special emphasis to, as this creates more obstacles to grasp to in the environment, better enabling you to avoid damage. Likewise, I found a jetpack in another run which allowed me to hover around.

Various characters are unlockable and each one has some uniqueness to them, with each level-up granting a percent increase to cooldown and the like. There's one character that is just a fly, and well, it flies around. It sort of delegitimizes the need for platforming, though each character has their own benefit.

All of this gameplay is under the guise of an intentionally sketchy, sort of rugged look. It's the visual style you'd associate with a developer like Messhof, unafraid of being a bit different, goofy and weird.

This demo version of Blood Dungeon fast-tracked the progress with a bunch of already-unlocked upgrades. Metaprogression like increases to stats are present, so that'll keep players coming back as the gameplay gradually becomes meatier and meatier after each session.

There's also various biomes, all with their own twist. Water with an oxygen gauge was something I had to contend with, for instance. There's also a daily run that refreshes every 24 hours, so that will definitely give me an incentive to come back and play Blood Dungeon again.

Blood Dungeon was previewed during Summer Game Fest 2026 Play Days.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/blood-dungeon-is-refreshing-spin-on-survivors-formula
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 Draws You Into An Action Packed Alien World With Fun Loadouts And Satisfying Combat
item.content:encoded:
One of the multiple Alien games that have made their appearance across Summer Game Fest is Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2. Leaning heavily into the back-half of an Alien film in this game, you and up to three friends will have boots on the ground to try to investigate a strange situation that evolves into a nightmare.

I sat down to play a mission about 1/3rd of the way through the game. Armed as a Specialist, the class focused on customization. We dropped into a mission where our team had noticed a ship that had gone dark on the far side of the planet. Dropping down, it wasn't long before the fireteam was getting swarmed by rogue synths and a whole bevy of Xenomorphs.

If you want to learn more about what has gone into creating the game, difficulty options, and how it was to work with 20th Century Fox check out our interview with Chris Cross, Studio Creative/Design Director at Cold Iron Studios

In terms of tone there definitely isn't the level of stress and tension that you might expect from the 'horror' style Alien games, instead Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 embodies that final act of an alien film where the monster has been seen and is no longer in the shadows, and you're ready to do everything you can to survive mowing your way through a large number of foes.

Before getting into the gameplay, we'll need to explain the classes a bit.

Loadout And Controls

Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 has six different classes that you can play as: the Duelist, Machinist, Marauder, Hunter, and Medic. There is an additional sixth class that I got to play as, the Specialist. This class is the jack of all trades that doesn't just have its own unique abilities, but is able to equip any of the abilities from the other classes.

Interestingly, the Specialist gets access to the abilities of the other classes once you've leveled up those classes enough. In this way, especially if you want to take on some of the more challenging difficulties, you'll want to practice with each of the classes, understand their mechanics and unlock their abilities, and then build a Specialist that caters very specifically to your playstyle.

During my time with the game, I was playing a Specialist who had access to an assault rifle, flamethrower, a powerful explosive rocket, and a grenade for lobbing.

Diving into the level, the action kicked off pretty much immediately as synths flooded into a large control room. I was a big fan of the unsettling blank expressions on the synth's faces and how some would swiftly jump over barriers to try to advance, while others almost tumbled.

They were almost zombie-like in their approach.

My squad, which comprised one of the developers and two AI companions, began to immediately mow down all of the enemies that came our way. As they poured in from different sides of the rooms in waves, there was a fun sense of covering one another's backs as you stopped seeing enemies and started hunting them out in the room.

As we dove deeper into the dilapidated facility, the waves of enemy synths were joined by xenomorphs crawling across the floors and ceilings, and exploding out of vents in the walls.

Variation in enemy design and their weaknesses

In combat, the Xenomorphs came in a variety of different varieties. The standard Xenomorph was human-sized and would crawl its way towards you; on top of that, there was a far larger variant that would hide in vents and pop out. There was also a variant that contained poison that you could spot with glowing spores on its head that, upon death, would pop, creating a hazard where it stood for some time.

Synths, too, had varieties, though these were pulling from the most commonly seen humanoid enemy variations. Some would approach with guns, some would have large shields, and there were even Synths that, after defeat, would trigger a bomb in their chest to explode, trying to take out any nearby Marines.

You can get through combat encounters with any weapons, but for the sake of conserving ammunition, there are also a variety of weapon types that can be more effective on certain enemies. When the Xenos started swarming, the flamethrower would make quick work of them, a downside of that being that your view would generally be crowded by fire. The Synths were especially weak to the Cryo weapons. Picking up a Cryo grenade, I was able to pitch it into a group of Synths and then shoot them, watching them shatter and hit the ground in a satisfying shower of parts.

The gun handling was incredibly satisfying. Short bursts from the assault rifle left Sync heads exploding or having them stumble from damaged limbs. 

There was such a stark and recognizable difference between the eerie pale light of the Synth, easy to see but coming at you like an overflowing wave, and the black carapace of the Aliens swarming towards you. Even if they weren't too difficult to kill at the difficulty level we were playing on, there was still a very real feeling that the swarm could overcome you.

Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 feels like a fast-paced final act to an Alien film

While only getting to play a short portion from the middle of Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2, I was incredible impressed with the world that had been built for the game, how satisfying the combat was along with the loadout crafting, and how much care had gone into ensuring that it would be a fun game to play with friends but also one you would have the satisfaction of playing again at new difficulties or with added challenges.

I can definitely see myself getting a few friends together in a voice chat to chat and joke our way through an adventure like this.

Aliens: FIreteam Elite 2 was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026 over the course of 30 minutes.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/aliens-fireteam-elite-2-draws-you-into-action-packed-alien-world-with-fun-loadouts
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Escape Academy 2 Is Co-Op Puzzle Perfection
item.content:encoded:
Every once in a while you have the fortune of running across a game that just feels made for you. Myself and colleague Andrew Stretch had a chance to check out Escape Academy 2, which showed that escape rooms in an open world is a match made in heaven.

If you want to learn even more about Escape Academy 2, check out our interview with Mike Salyh, Co-Founder of Coin Crew Games.

The very essence of escape room design is exploration and environmental storytelling, which Escape Academy 2 embraces fully. There is something to see just about everywhere you look and a reward for creatively checking around every corner.

That rewarding exploration bears out in the world and lore of Escape Academy 2 as well. Where the story and lore played a small role in the first, developer Coin Crew Games wanted the characters and academy to have equal footing with the gameplay this time around.

The academy is full of people, from the wacky professors to other students around campus. The professors are the real drivers of the story and larger than life, but there are a ton of students with side quests/puzzles that range from something as simple as get me a bag of chips to figuring out some elaborate puzzle they themselves have devised. 

Our short time made us feel like we were apart of the academy as well. In one quest, we had to collect random items like a toy boat and train for a professor. Those items then showed up in the following escape room puzzle we had to complete.

Where Escape Academy 2 really shines is in its co-op experience. I am certain I would have enjoyed my time with the game if I had played it by myself, but it is such an amazing co-op experience that it really feels like the most ideal way to play.

And I don't mean that in the sense that two brains are better than one, but that the simple ways in which you can more easily work with someone is a joy. For example, your partner looking at a list of symbols and relaying information while you're inputting them into keypad to unlock a door.

It's so natural to just fall into a rhythm and communicate what you see and are doing, which is oddly rare with co-op games. Too often, games are about just simply existing in the same space where Escape Academy 2 seamlessly lets you work collaboratively. 

The puzzle design we were able to interact with was just delightful as well. There is a logic to everything, and everyday objects work just how you would think. Interacting with any object felt natural and realistic in the sense that I was never caught off guard by what I anticipated a certain object to do.

On top of that, some aspects of a puzzle are not signposted to you at all but are part of the natural environment. It's easy to see that the doors with weird symbols above them are a puzzle, but maybe not so much with a clipboard with some writing on it.

One puzzle had a solution hidden in just that, a normal clipboard with the text of a report on a failed science experiment. Reading it gave us the information we needed to read a chart that then corresponded with a combination lock on it.

We ended our session with a more advanced escape room that had a 30-minute timer attached to it for completion. There were a ton of interlocking puzzles that were all connected by some crazy Rube Goldberg machine whose goal was to get a rat through a series of tubes so it'd finally reach a wheel to power a light.

Within that room, which was split in two, there were more than ten unique puzzles, all of which were very different from one another, to solve. Some were connected, needing to solve one to then get access to the next, some were more standalone, but all worked toward the goal.

For example, one really cool solution was tied to some colored paper airplanes you could launch in the air. Each only traveled a certain distance, and the order of those colored planes from closest to furthest was a key component to finding out how to unlock something.

There are a ton of examples I could go into, but suffice to say Escape Academy 2 is great at giving you triumphant moment after triumphant moment as you solve a particular puzzle or unlock a door. The next serotonin dump is always just around the corner.

Escape Academy 2 Preview | Final Thoughts

In total, Escape Academy 2 is aiming to be about a 15-hour game with a ton of puzzles, a mystery about the academy itself, and a lot of charm in its characters. The open world of the academy grounds is great, and the moment-to-moment gameplay of puzzling was wonderful.

Escape Academy 2 is joining the crowded month of September for releases, but I can tell you that myself and Andrew Stretch will be keeping a close eye on this. It's a no-brainer for puzzle fans, particularly if you liked things like Blue Prince or Animal Well.

Escape Academy 2 was previewed at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/escape-academy-2-preview-co-op-puzzle-perfection
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Escape Academy 2 Emphasizes The Academy With School-Wide Open World Puzzles - Interview
item.content:encoded:
At Summer Game Fest, Coin Crew Games was ready to show off the latest build of Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School. After getting to experience some of the open world gameplay of the academy, complete some of the unique side-puzzles, and help a rat make its way through a series of puzzles to power a lightbulb, we got to sit with Mike Salyh, Co-Founder of Coin Crew Games, to discuss puzzle design, how the sequel has grown, and how they're making sure that anyone can play Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School.

If you want to learn more about our time with Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School you can check out Andrew Otton's preview.

In the initial Escape Academy, you would move from escape room to escape room, selecting them on the map. While the landscape and structure of the academy were present, it was simply a map to cursor through. Having had a chance to walk, interact, and even platform through the open world, I was interested to know what had given Coin Crew games the push to expand the world so much.

To understand the proper scale of Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School, Salyh told us that while the first game should take a player about 4 hours to complete, the sequel should take players 15 hours.

More Academy to complement the Escape

"When we made the first Escape Academy, we really liked the world we had built for the game," Salyh explained, "All of the characters and puzzle humor. Let's say the first game was 90% Escape and 10% academy, so when we wanted to make a sequel, we wanted to get closer to 50% escape and 50% academy."

From our exploration of the open world, we didn't just travel from location to location but ran into other students looking for our help that required a puzzle to be solved to proceed, hidden environmental elements, and more. At one point, while exploring, I had ended up platforming up to the top of a building, and even higher to the top of some stove pipe towers.

"I wanted to give you more opportunities to run around the school, talk to other students, and find secrets. This lent itself to this open-world style of gameplay."

There's been a big trend for puzzle games recently to push players to solve puzzles outside of the game. When asking for the range of puzzles from the first Escape Academy to recent meta-puzzlers like Animal Well, Blue Prince, or Tunic, Salyh first shared his own love of those titles before explaining where Escape Academy 2 falls on the spectrum.

"I really love all of the games on that list. With this game, we were focused a bit more on 'Escape Rooms'. There are definitely some secrets that span across the entire school, and there's at least one puzzle we put in there where we were like 'this is an Easter Egg of an Easter Egg.'"

Escape Academy 2 Is Making Sure All Players Can Complete The Game

The puzzles that we completed relied on us to listen to chirping birds, understand links between paper airplanes and symbols, and watch light cast from a prism. I was interested to hear how puzzle design was implemented for those with limited hearing or color blindness.

"This is one of my favorite parts of the process, we have a Discord where people can sign up to playtest, and so getting a lot of players to play through it's been insightful. We have a colorblind mode in the game, where, when you turn it on, for any color-specific puzzle, it will include a label saying what color it is."

Continuing to explain some of the accessibility options, Salyh said: "We have a similar thing for audio-specific puzzles where you can turn on closed captions."

One of the puzzles that we completed required us to listen carefully, but due to the busy nature of Summer Game Fest, it was hard to hear exactly what was going on but we did notice a hint to our solution in the captions. What allowed for the solution of the puzzle, about correctly describing a bird who wakes early, I was able to solve based on my knowledge of birds.

On hearing that Salyh was happy to hear, he explained that they always wanted to make sure that if a player had knowledge about a certain subject, it could help them solve puzzles. "If it's a subject you know, we want to reward you. We don't try to trust outside knowledge, but if you've got it, then it should count and help you."

Something that I definitely had to compliment Escape Academy 2 on was its approach to split-screen, treating both players as separate entities. It was really refreshing in a co-op scenario that when the other player is interacting with a character for a quest or going through a puzzle, the other player is left to freely roam.

On top of that, if the other party member happens to be in conversation with an NPC, you can see the speech bubbles of their conversation.

Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School will be released in September 2026.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/escape-academy-2-emphasizes-academy-with-school-wide-open-world-puzzles-interview
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Blood Message is a Brutal, Cinematic Experience
item.content:encoded:
Summer Game Fest 2026 was a resounding success, with plenty of exciting trailers from beloved IPs and developers. The one that blew me away the most, however, was most unexpected: Blood Message.

Set during the Tang Dynasty, the Golden Age of China, Blood Message offers an unrelenting, bloody, unglorified look at war and conflict. After getting my hands on it at Summer Game Fest's Play Days, I am convinced this is cooking up to be something special.

Developed by 24 Entertainment and published by NetEase, this is the first hands-on demo offered to media. I was eager to play this myself, since while the trailers thus far were incredible beautiful and cinematic, I really wanted to learn more about its gameplay.

But the thing that stood out to me first and foremost was the visuals, which is as beautiful as the trailers themselves. The setting of our gameplay session is in a settlement in the northwestern region of China. The terrain is rugged, sandy, and unapologetic.

A fitting location, then, for a game with combat just as brutal as the deserts it's set within. We begin by searching through a village. It's under attack by an unknown group of soldiers, and as I understand it, we're searching for our son with our brother.

Blood Message starts calm, but soon all hell breaks loose. Our brother gets in a tussle with a group of soldiers. I immediately rush to save him as other soldiers close in. Light attacks are fast and vicious, almost feral in a sense.

You're able to execute wounded enemies, slamming your cold steel on their necks. Again, it's a brutal and honest look at combat. Along with the light attacks are heavy attacks. What impressed me is the protagonist taking on a halfsword technique (essentially, grabbing the blade of the sword with one hand and the hilt with the other for extra weight and power).

These attacks slice through enemy shields and I mixed them with light attacks to take out my foes. There is a parry, which offers a generous window and staggers enemies. Once they're staggered, a light attack is a good option to finish them off.

Here's where Blood Message really shines, though. I haven't seen a game seamlessly blend cinematic and real-time, player-controlled combat quite like this. Enemies will grab you from behind and you'll have to mash a button to get out of their grip; meanwhile, swords will clash and sometimes you need to overpower your opponent in this struggle.

If you look at the trailer for Blood Message, you'll see that everything looks scripted. I was trying to interpret what the real gameplay was like, and as the trailer shows, it's actually quite accurate. You feel like you're in a cutscenes while throwing out these attacks.

When you think about swordplay in video games, it's actually quite a bit more sanitized than reality. Two humans are struggling to take each other's lives, and that's certainly reflected in the weight of our movement.

We also partake in several stealth segments, though if you are discovered you can just fight it out and hope to survive. Stealth kills are just as visceral, with the protagonist using a hammer to mercilessly end these soldiers.

Eventually, we're caught in a scripted sequence and have to escape. Soldiers are chasing us as arrows whizz by our head, and more foes are trying to intercept me on the way forward. Dashing through houses, crashing through scaffolding and jumping from rooftops, all I can do is run forward and keep going.

Our chase is interrupted by an enemy crashing into us from the left, pinning the protagonist against the wall. A brutal struggle ensues as I frantically tap square to stop him from plunging his dagger into my shoulder. It still stabs me despite the struggle, but I soon overpower the soldier and shove the dagger into his eye.

Overall, I get the sense that Blood Message is a linear experience, and perhaps even a shorter one. Even so, if it can maintain its strong momentum with such strong, compelling, cinematic scenes and combat encounters, I am all in.

The narrative, while hard to intuit from the demo, involves father and son, which is relatable even for those who aren't familiar with China's history. In that regard, I think it'll be easy to get invested, and it might even tug at my heart strings.

Blood Message was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026 Play Days.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/blood-message-is-brutal-cinematic-experience
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
